Title: Post-translational coordination of chlorophyll biosynthesis and breakdown by BCMs maintains chlorophyll homeostasis during leaf development

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: photosynthetic light harvesting; chloroplast establishment; avoidance of phototoxic free chlorophyll accumulation; nutrient remobilization during senescence
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: young and mature rosette leaves; chloroplast thylakoid-associated photosystems
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: integral chloroplast membrane scaffold protein; N-terminal chloroplast transit peptide; six transmembrane domains
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast thylakoid membrane, enriched in stroma lamellae/non-appressed regions; minor envelope fraction
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: accumulates in all tissues except roots; highest in young and mature rosette leaves; trace in etiolated seedlings and rapidly induced by illumination; decreases strongly during senescence
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: young green leaves and during de-etiolation; reduced during senescence
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light induction after etiolation; varying light regimes; dark-induced senescence
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Arabidopsis null mutants; overexpression; double mutants with gun5 and gun4; BCM2 complementation in bcm1; VIGS; Y2H; BiFC; co-immunoprecipitation; in vitro Mg-chelatase assay with thylakoids and recombinant proteins; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; metabolite profiling by HPLC; immunoblotting
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes chlorophyll biosynthesis and suppresses chlorophyll degradation, thereby maintaining chlorophyll homeostasis; bcm1 causes pale-green leaves and accelerated chlorophyll loss during senescence, whereas overexpression confers cosmetic stay-green
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BCM1 is an intrinsic thylakoid membrane protein required for normal chlorophyll accumulation in developing leaves. 2. BCM1 stimulates Mg-chelatase activity through interaction with GUN4, increasing Mg-porphyrin formation and chlorophyll biosynthesis. 3. BCM1 also interacts with SGR1 and reduces SGR1 abundance, thereby attenuating chlorophyll breakdown during dark-induced senescence.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for tuning chlorophyll homeostasis and stay-green traits; overexpression may improve chlorophyll retention during senescence, but deployment should consider delayed chlorophyll breakdown and possible effects on nutrient remobilization
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression causes cosmetic stay-green during senescence, which may potentially delay chlorophyll turnover/nutrient remobilization though senescence-associated gene induction still occurs
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Public Arabidopsis co-expression datasets/ATTED-II used for candidate identification; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts post-translationally rather than by altering transcription of chlorophyll biosynthesis or catabolic genes; proposed scaffold protein coordinating antagonistic chlorophyll anabolic and catabolic pathways

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: photosynthetic light harvesting; chlorophyll turnover during senescence; nutrient remobilization during leaf senescence
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BCM paralog; integral chloroplast membrane scaffold protein with extended N-terminus relative to BCM1; predicted transmembrane protein
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast membrane-associated protein; exact suborganellar localization not directly shown here
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expression resembles chlorophyll catabolic genes such as SGR1 and PAO; highly expressed in senescent leaves and dry seeds; low in young seedlings
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: up-regulated during senescence; low in young leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockdown; bcm1 bcm2 double mutant; VIGS silencing; constitutive overexpression in bcm1 background; Y2H/BiFC interaction evidence via supplementary data; dark-induced senescence assays; HPLC metabolite profiling; immunoblotting
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: functionally substitutes for BCM1 in promoting chlorophyll biosynthesis when overexpressed, and negatively regulates chlorophyll breakdown during senescence; loss together with bcm1 enhances chlorophyll loss in old leaves
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BCM2 is a conserved BCM paralog with expression preferentially associated with senescence. 2. Constitutive BCM2 expression rescues bcm1 chlorophyll-biosynthesis defects, indicating functional conservation with BCM1. 3. BCM2 attenuates chlorophyll degradation during senescence and contributes with BCM1 to restrain SGR1-mediated catabolism.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to engineer stay-green or chlorophyll-retention traits, particularly during senescence; may be useful to modulate late-stage chlorophyll loss
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced chlorophyll retention/stay-green may interfere with chlorophyll turnover and remobilization during senescence
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Public Arabidopsis co-expression datasets/ATTED-II used for expression comparison; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Protein accumulation in senescent WT leaves is very low and detectable mainly in overexpression lines; authors propose BCM2 acts predominantly at late developmental stages
